posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
• 2015 K Vintners Syrah The Beautiful - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley, Walla Walla Valley (10/29/2018)
    Day 1: Not what I would expect from Washington State. Very Northern Rhone. White pepper, game, violets, earth and black licorice. This has a medium to long finish and is quite a complex wine. Not many would pick Washington State in a blind tasting. I am quite please with this day 1 showing on quite a young wine. Should evolve on day 2, 3 and possibly 4. 94 points
    Day 2: Violets, white pepper, soy sauce, game and menthol. This has the least amount of fruit I have ever tasted from a Washington State Syrah but I love this wine! One of the more interesting wines of the year for me. A must buy! 95 points
    Day 3: Holding form from day 1 and 2. I love this wine.
    Recommendation: Buy a three or four and try one young and see how this evolves over the years. I see this as a 15 year wine. One of my top wines of the year considering the price of $45. (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Gary Ahearn
posts.cooked: Thanks for the note, John. I bought 4 bottles last week. This was our favorite producer on a recent trip to Walla Walla. And when I saw that this wine had garnered 98 pts. from William Kelley, well . . . a no brainer.

posts.name: C. Keller
posts.cooked: I’ve had a lot of this producer and love it. I had a 2015 The Hidden about a month ago. You could smell it from the decanter across the room. I’m having a hard time keeping my hands off of the other bottle I have. I see my local store has this one so I may have to get a bottle this weekend,

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The wines have certainly changed a lot from just a handful of years ago. Native ferments, long but gentle macerations with very little new wood and lots of savory complexity are now the order of the day.

posts.name: Brandon Redman
posts.cooked: Great note, John, thanks for posting.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
The wines have certainly changed a lot from just a handful of years ago. Native ferments, long but gentle macerations with very little new wood and lots of savory complexity are now the order of the day.

What vintage did you notice the change?
Cheers,

John

posts.name: Chris Ewen
posts.cooked: Just had a 2014 powerline. 1st day was overwhelming stewed fruit. 2nd day better but no way near cellar tracker notes. Maybe had an off bottle.

The wines have certainly changed a lot from just a handful of years ago. Native ferments, long but gentle macerations with very little new wood and lots of savory complexity are now the order of the day.

This is great to hear, I will give K another chance.

And btw Mr. Kelley, if it hasn’t been mentioned enough, we greatly appreciate your contributions.

posts.name: Randy Bowman
posts.cooked: Had a 13 K Vintners Morrison Vineyard Syrah last night. Big, bold and beautiful. Across the K Vintners portfolio the wines are stellar and age very well. The last three wines we’ve had from K Vintners, The Boy, Guido and Motor City Kitty were all outstanding. If I could remember more than two weeks ago, we probably had others. Charles enjoys making wine and loves to play around with the blend now and then but they are always tasty. Great notes John.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked: Very encouraging note. I did a horizontal of 2010s for my wife’s birthday (cougar hills, hidden, beautiful, old stones) and I remember each being distinctive, interesting (with beautiful and hidden especially tasty); but I wouldn’t have characterized them as restrained, complex, or very reminiscent of N. Rhône style. This makes me want to revisit. Has the price dropped some as well? Several bottlings were $60-75 at that time…

posts.name: Randy Bowman
posts.cooked: Where are you buying the wines John? They aren’t that expensive on the west coast.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked: Texas, and this was 2.5 years ago(!)

posts.name: Randy Bowman
posts.cooked: Now I know why I can’t ship to Texas. Somebody in government there makes good money on wine sales to you and other Texans.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Where are you buying the wines John? They aren’t that expensive on the west coast.

Minneapolis. Wine Searcher showed this starting at $50 at Wine Exchange and up.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked: Bought two more today!

posts.name: Peter Panzica
posts.cooked:
My local Whole Foods gets a fair bit of K. Picked up 3 each of 2015 Cattle King and Powerline. With 10% off a 6 pack plus the 5% Amazon gives you not bad on the price. Based on WK’s note I should have picked up some of The Creator too.
Also had 13s and 14s of Cattle King and Hidden sitting in the cellar.
Had a 13 Klein last night which was solid.
Any opinions on when these wines will be in their optimal window?

posts.name: Will G Pleure
posts.cooked: K Vinters makes a lot of great wine

The wines have certainly changed a lot from just a handful of years ago. Native ferments, long but gentle macerations with very little new wood and lots of savory complexity are now the order of the day.

Big +1. The past few years have been much more expressive of Syrah’s savory potential and a little less lavish fruit and oak.

IIRC, there has been some turnover in assistant winemakers, which does have some influence here. I’m hoping thare will be some continuity of style.

posts.name: Jon B A R T
posts.cooked: Just picked up 3 bottles of the 2015 Powerline from wine.com. Loved the bottle of 2014 that I tried, so this Syrah is an annual purchase for me at this point.

link_counts.title: Wine.com - Buy Wine Online - Wine & Wine Gifts Delivered to You

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: I bought an array of '07’s a number of years ago. Each one was a volatile oaky mess and just reminded me of the worst of Aussie shiraz. Never got around to the Wailuke Slope and don’t think I ever will. Interesting that they’ve changed direction but there is so much great N.Rhone that I think I’ll just stick to that. Yes, call me an elitist snob but once bitten, twice shy.
